posts.caption:
This is not a lecture on fiscal virtue. But fashion, historically, is rarely good at recognising when the mood changes. And in India, the mood seems to be shifting. Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s recent SpendSmart call for economic patriotism—buy less, buy wisely, postpone foreign holidays, suspend non-essential gold purchases, choose India-made products and even “wed in India”—arrives at a moment when India is showing off spectacular consumption. The Met Gala “wealth and style” discourse has barely subsided when paid-for visibility at Cannes and chatter around forthcoming couture weddings and shows is surging. Even Indian pret is on its own trip—garments are priced anywhere between ₹20,000 and ₹60,000.
Designers insist that all is well; even shirts priced upwards of ₹30,000 sell. Luxury managers and marketers, however, have a different refrain: all is “in” the well.
If India takes SpendSmart seriously—and signs suggest it already is—fashion’s current excess may begin to look oddly out of step. Reports of companies curbing foreign travel, conserving energy, oil and resources already point to a recalibration.
Fashion’s mood, though, appears entirely contrary. At Cannes, even aspirant celebrities reportedly pay staggering sums to walk the red carpet and be photographed. Across fashion and celebrity culture, there is an almost stubborn insistence that nothing is wrong with the world. Even when geopolitical instability is rattling markets, foreign investment remains unsettled and the rupee has weakened sharply against the dollar.
If India begins applauding strategic spending, will fashion—which is by nature capitalist and aspirational—begin to appear tone-deaf in all its excesses?
Which raises the question. Is economic patriotism merely about buying and wedding in India? Or will SpendSmart demand something tougher: downsizing lavishness and embracing strategic restraint? Fashion survives on visibility and money. It sells fantasy for a living. If the cultural mood shifts toward prudence, will fashion’s business model be compromised if it tones itself down?
In this current economic mood, what might a middle path look like? Tell us what you think.
🖋️@shefatwork

posts.caption:
Right to Be Seen Travels to London Craft Week
The Right to Be Seen: Reframing the Passport Photo in Craft, conceptualised by The Voice of Fashion, created in collaboration with Royal Enfield Social Mission and curated by Juhi Pandey, has now travelled to the London Craft Week. Presented as part of Royal Enfield’s The Himalayan Knot showcase at the The Friday Sari Project in Hampstead, the broader project reframes identity, visibility and representation through the lens of handmade practice.
At the London showcase, visitors will encounter portraits of artisan Raziya Bano from Kargil, whose work offers a meditation on labour and belonging in the Himalayan region. Originally exhibited in December 2025 at Journeying Across the Himalayas, Royal Enfield’s multidisciplinary cultural festival in Delhi, the project now travels to an international audience at a moment when conversations around craft are increasingly tied to authorship, ethics and recognition.
The Right to Be Seen series asks a simple question: what happens when artisans create their own portraits using the very skills that define their craft traditions? Built around the idea of the identity photograph, the project invites makers to reinterpret their own faces through embroidery, weaving and textile-based hand techniques, shifting them from anonymous custodians of craft to visible authors of culture.
Part exhibition, part textile conservation initiative, The Himalayan Knot brings together capsule collections shaped by indigenous knowledge systems and contemporary design language. Among the highlights are The Fifth Gesture, created by label MARGN with the women artisans of self-help group Jomche in Himachal Pradesh and Higher Plains and Suket Dhir’s collaboration with Looms of Ladakh, which merges pashmina with jamdani techniques in earthy shades drawn from Indian madder and marigold.
🖋️ @anannya_s_

posts.caption: You cleverly use progressive language but without viewing things through the lens of class, it stops from being a true feminist discussion. Alia is out there wearing processes couture a day after Indians were told to tighten their belts, to brace for hardships, to not use petrol, to refrain from travelling. She boarded a flight and went t Europe when Indians were asked not to fly. It’s like there is a different set of rules for the hyper privileged and a different India for them. It’s not restrained glamour at all - it’s laughing at the have-nots. It’s exploitative. It’s hiding behind girl-boss language but never expressing class solidarity. When Indians like Alia got on such global stages - exactly who are they representing? The other elites? Or, are they representing the struggling masses who can’t afford the most basic necessities. Fashion without context is just colours. Feminism without intersectionality is nothing more than an excuse for bad behavior.

posts.caption:
Spinster was a legal term in England until 2005 😟
Not ancient history or a relic of the far off, distant past.
Women currently alive were referred to in official legal documents by a medieval textile job title that had been weaponised into an insult.
Because that’s what a spinster was originally. A job title for a woman who spins wool into yarn. A job that involved skilled labour and was essential to the entire pre-industrial economy.
Then unmarried women got pushed out of better-paid textile work (because they didn’t have husbands with trade connections) and into spinning. The lowest-status work that was left for them.
So the job title became a proxy for marital status and by the 17th century it was the legal term for an unmarried woman in official documents.
Full episode on The Culture of Cloth 🎧 Ep 3: The Dictionary Was Named After a Woman Weaver. Link in bio.

posts.caption:
My married surname is Tucker. I’ve been using it for years without knowing what it meant.
Tucker comes from the Old English tucian which means to torment or beat cloth. A tucker was a cloth finisher: someone who took raw woven fabric off the loom and scoured it, beat it, trampled it in water until it became finished cloth ready to wear.
It was skilled physical labour that was essential to the entire textile trade.
The same job had three names depending on where you were in England. Fuller in the north. Walker in Scotland and the Midlands. Tucker in the West Country…Devon, Somerset, Wiltshire, Dorset, Cornwall.
My husband’s patrilineal family came from Plymouth and Cornwall.
The name came from exactly the place where it originated, where the cloth trade shaped the landscape and the language for centuries.
And Cornwall isn’t simply the far end of England, Kernow is one of the six Celtic nations with a textile tradition that predates the Norman conquest by centuries.
I am a textile and fashion designer and I married into the name of a cloth worker from a Celtic textile region without knowing any of this.
This beautiful man and his name found me 🫶🏼😭
Tucker is one surname and there are dozens more carrying the same secret and I’m going deep on more of them in the next episode of my podcast, The Culture of Cloth 🎧 Link in bio.

posts.caption:
The oldest woven garment in the world sat in a storage pile for sixty years. And not because it was secretly hidden but because nobody thought to even look at it.
Excavated by Flinders Petrie in 1913 from a First Dynasty tomb south of Cairo and then in 1977 a conservator followed a seam, found another stitched to it, then another and realised three rags were a shirt with two sleeves.
Radiocarbon dating confirmed its age in 2016: between 3482 and 3102 BCE. It is more than five thousand years old. That means there’s more than a hundred years between excavation and confirmation.
The dress is Egyptian. It is in London.
Excavated during the period of British colonial administration of Egypt and held there ever since.
The Tarkhan Dress is pleated and it was buried with someone who valued it enough to take it with them.
Part of an ongoing series on fashion, history, and whose stories get told.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

“I don’t want to understand everyone; I want a world where I live and let others live. In New York City, I was staying in the middle of a huge building, surrounded by people of different races and religions, and there was no tension whatsoever. Sloterdijk even talks about how we need to learn a certain kind of discretion. That’s what bothers me — the idea that I have to understand your world. How am I supposed to understand your world when neither you understand it nor I understand my own? I’m very superficial in that regard. You have a neighbor — establish some kind of order, with basic respect, and don’t push yourself onto them, nor let them push themselves onto you. I take great pleasure in discretion. Maybe that comes with age.”

posts.caption:
Two days after the official drop date, the hype behind the Audemars Piguet x Swatch Royal Pop collection remains strong. Today, @VogueBusiness was on the ground in London to see the continued consumer pull towards the £335 pocket watches.
In the morning, queues wrapped around the block at both Oxford Street and Covent Garden flagship stores with people waiting to get their hands on the limited-edition drop. Only 30 watches were delivered to Oxford Street today; they sold out instantly on a first-come, first-served basis. Many were still hopeful later on in the afternoon, but were met with temporarily closed storefronts and a police presence following the initial morning crowds.
The majority of interested buyers were resellers, some buying on behalf of their bosses, with groups of friends, others with parents, or independently. One anonymous bystander said, “I went to White City on Saturday morning, and people didn’t really know how to act. It’s excessive. There were kids in buggies, grown people and elderly people.”
Video and reporting: @_aureliarachel

posts.caption:
The women’s world number one @ArynaSabalenka understands that tennis can be opera: full of triumph and despair, grace and slapstick, sin and atonement. The 28-year-old has been at the top of her sport for the last year and a half, with a deep bench of brand associations—including with Gucci, Nike, and Audemars Piguet—to show for it. But since her earliest days on the tour, she’s been a fiery on-court presence, known for her outbursts (she’s a world-class racket-smasher) and tense exchanges with officials. (She attributes much of this to being “very much a Taurus”).
Time and training have helped her to temper that fire—but it also has its uses. “This is part of my personality that can drive me crazy, but it can also drive me into that real fight mode and help me play with passion,” Sabalenka says. “It’s two sides of the medal.”
For Vogue’s May 2026 digital cover, @robertjhaskell meets Aryna Sabalenka in Rome to talk winning, losing, grieving, learning, and never letting up.

These days she can take that information and process it better," says her fitness trainer, Jason Stacy. "It’s the progression from fighter to warrior. She was that young fighter, running off adrenaline, surviving the moment. Warriors are calmer. They can zoom out and then zoom back in and refocus on the right thing.”

posts.caption: There’s a new sound coming out of Norway that’s like nothing you’ve ever heard before. It’s produced by twenty-two year old rappers HC (@hchiace) and Subaru (@zubaro111), who perform together as Bygdetrapen, and who have created a sub genre called rural trap. The duo’s focus is on the hyper local and personal. Their subject is life in their small town in the Telemark region, where skiing originated. (Think snowmobiles, cars, inside jokes, friends.) Most radically, they sing in their local dialect, which has roots in Old Norse. “Our stuff is kind of only made to be understood by a couple hundred people who know the area we’re from,” said Subaru, yet their music has a growing appeal to masses of fans (like @the_lbp) who can’t understand what  Bygdetrapen is saying, but cannot stop listening. Tune in to the new sound by tapping the link in bio.

@maudeapatow is ready now. Fresh off her return in the highly anticipated third season of #Euphoria, Apatow is stepping into a new chapter with the kind of clarity and control that only comes from growing up in real time—on-screen, off-screen, and alongside co-stars who have gone on to navigate intergalactic levels of fame and careers of their own. "Just thinking about us being little kids and having no idea what was in store to now… It's insane," she says.
In our May cover story, Apatow opens up about the roles that shaped her, the relationships that fulfilled her, the theater work that grounded her, and the rituals—travel, matcha, and all—that keep her moving forward. Read our full interview with the actress and director at the link in our bio.

The @swatch x @audemarspiguet Royal Pop collaboration is already showing signs of volatility on the secondary market, with resale prices dropping sharply from their initial peaks, while still holding above retail.⁠
⁠
Early transactions surged in the immediate aftermath of Saturday’s release.⁠
⁠
On Sunday, the multicolor-on-white Huit Blanc model sold for as much as 2,022 euros before falling to current listings around $1,295. A similar pattern played out with the blue Savonnette Lan Ba colorway, which reached 4,310 euros at its peak the same day and is now listed closer to 1,431 euros.⁠
⁠
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: @renan_botelho, @lilytempleton

posts.caption:
Cannes tends to favor drama not only on the screen, but the fashion kind, too — of trains, sequins and architectural gowns.⁠
⁠
For her latest appearance on the red carpet, @rileykeough was drawn to something softer.⁠
⁠
For her the choice was simple. “It was just one of the most beautiful things I’ve ever seen in my life,” she says of the wisp-thin and delicate skirt combo.⁠
⁠
It was an atypical red carpet selection. Instead of a voluminous gown, this was almost impossibly light. But the choice fit neatly into the mood currently surrounding @chanelofficial — a house Keough has long orbited — since @matthieu_blazy has taken it in a new direction.⁠
⁠
Read more about Keough's take on Chanel's "whole new era," growing older in Hollywood and working opposite Barry Keoghan at WWD.com — #linkinbio⁠
⁠
Report: @rhondarichford⁠
Photos: Marina Martinez / WWD
